nodes.title: Model active resources explicitly
nodes.why: The demo needs observable resource ownership so tests can assert one active connection while mounted and zero after cleanup.
nodes.alternative: Track only render counts or setup call counts.
nodes.tradeoff: A resource set is test-oriented scaffolding, but it verifies the lifecycle behavior that matters.

nodes.title: Make each connection setup idempotent
nodes.why: A connection object should not register multiple active tokens if connect is accidentally called twice.
nodes.alternative: Add a token every time connect runs.
nodes.tradeoff: The guard adds local state to the connection object, but it prevents duplicate resource accounting.

nodes.title: Disconnect the exact resource that connected
nodes.why: Cleanup must remove the token created by this connection and report the new active count.
nodes.alternative: Clear all active connections or skip cleanup when StrictMode runs an extra cycle.
nodes.tradeoff: Per-resource cleanup is more precise, and it keeps multiple mounted instances from interfering with each other.

nodes.title: Let the effect own one reversible process
nodes.why: Each effect setup creates and connects one resource, and the returned cleanup disconnects that same resource.
nodes.alternative: Use a has-run ref to suppress the second StrictMode setup.
nodes.tradeoff: React may do extra development work, but the mounted steady state remains correct without hiding lifecycle defects.

nodes.title: Depend on the reactive room and callback
nodes.why: Changing rooms should clean up the old connection before creating a new one, while the stable state setter keeps the callback dependency safe.
nodes.alternative: Use an empty dependency array or suppress the hooks lint rule.
nodes.tradeoff: Correct dependencies can rerun the effect on real synchronization changes, but they avoid stale resources.

nodes.title: Keep unrelated renders outside the effect inputs
nodes.why: The re-render button proves that state unrelated to the connection does not create another active resource.
nodes.alternative: Tie every render counter or UI state value to the effect dependencies.
nodes.tradeoff: Separating effect inputs from display-only state requires discipline, but it keeps synchronization scoped.
